translatedText: Another edition of Les Fleurs du mal was published with 6 poems deleted and a significant number of poems added.
Attempted to run for the Académie Française, but withdrew due to criticism from critic Charles Augustin Sainte-Beuve.
Due to debt, he went to Brussels to publish books and give lectures, but the publishing industry was indifferent.
Died in Paris in August.
On May 11, the 6 deleted poems from Les Fleurs du mal were acquitted and republished in France.
Activities as a Critic and Poet
Contributed to the literary world by working as a French critic and poet.
Winning Literary Competitions
Showed early literary talent by winning an honorable mention in Latin poetry and a second prize in French poetry in national competitions.
Publication of Salon of 1845
Attracted attention for his audacity by publishing his first art criticism work, Salon of 1845.
Publication of the Novella La Fanfarlo
Continued his activities as a writer by publishing the novella La Fanfarlo.
Translation of Edgar Allan Poe's Works
Translated Edgar Allan Poe's works and introduced them to French readers.
Publication of Les Fleurs du mal
Published his first and most famous poetry collection, Les Fleurs du mal (The Flowers of Evil), in 1857.
Later Literary Activities

Published works such as prose poems to be included in Le Spleen de Paris (Paris Spleen), L'Œuvre et la vie d'Eugène Delacroix (The Work and Life of Delacroix), and Le Peintre de la vie moderne (The Painter of Modern Life).
Revered as the Father of Symbolism
Revered as the father of Symbolism, praised by Stéphane Mallarmé and Paul Verlaine.
What was Charles Baudelaire's profession?
He was a French critic and poet.
What is Charles Baudelaire's most famous work?
His most famous work is his first poetry collection, Les Fleurs du mal (The Flowers of Evil), published in 1857.
What controversy surrounded the publication of Les Fleurs du mal?
Les Fleurs du mal was prosecuted for offending public morals on the grounds of being unwholesome, resulting in a fine, but it was acquitted in 1949.
Which author's works did Charles Baudelaire translate?
He translated the works of Edgar Allan Poe, whose short stories had a profound influence on him.
Which art movement did Charles Baudelaire influence?
He is revered as the father of Symbolism, praised by Stéphane Mallarmé and Paul Verlaine.
December 4, 1866
Born in Moscow.
Settled in Munich at the age of 30 and began studying at a private school and the Academy of Fine Arts.
Ended his cohabitation with the German Expressionist female painter Gabriele Münter.
Returned to Moscow after the Russian Revolution.
Resigned his professorship at the Moscow Academy of Fine Arts and returned to Germany.
Began teaching students at the Bauhaus.
Published his second theoretical work, Point and Line to Plane.
Traveled to Egypt, Greece, and Turkey.
Moved to France after the Bauhaus was closed due to political pressure.
Acquired French citizenship.
December 13, 1944
Died in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France.
Founder of Abstract Painting
Evaluated as the founder of abstract painting, he is known as the artist who painted the first modern abstract works.
Theoretical Work Point and Line to Plane
Published his second theoretical work, Point and Line to Plane, in 1926, describing the relationship between the basic formative elements of painting.
Role at the Bauhaus
In charge of the mural workshop at the Bauhaus, and later earned trust as a deputy director by fostering clear judgment in students.
Catalyst for Transitioning to Abstraction
Listening to Wagner's opera Lohengrin and experiencing synesthesia—seeing colors while listening to music—served as a crucial turning point for his transition to abstract art.
How is Kandinsky evaluated as an artist?
He is evaluated as the founder of abstract painting and is one of the most important artists of the 20th century, comparable to Picasso and Matisse.
When and where was Kandinsky born and when did he die?
He was born in Moscow on December 4, 1866, and died in Neuilly-sur-Seine, France, on December 13, 1944.
What is Kandinsky's major theoretical work?
His major theoretical works include Point and Line to Plane, published in 1926.
What was the catalyst for Kandinsky's transition to abstract painting?
Experiencing synesthesia—seeing colors while listening to music—upon hearing Wagner's opera Lohengrin was a crucial catalyst for his transition to abstract art.
Born in Paris on June 7.
Moved to Peru with his family.
Left Peru and moved to Orléans, France, after his guardian's downfall.
Entered a naval preparatory school and later served as a pilot and in the navy.
Returned to Paris and got a job at a brokerage firm.
Married a Danish woman, Mette-Sophie Gad, and began painting as a hobby.
Moved to a house with a studio in the Vaugirard district.
Submitted works to the Impressionist exhibition.
Quit his career as a stockbroker due to the financial crisis and transitioned to a full-time painter.
In October, requested help from Camille Pissarro as a full-time painter.
Moved to Rouen with his family, and in November, the entire family relocated to Copenhagen.
Returned to Paris in June with his son Clovis.
In May, submitted 19 paintings and 1 wood relief to the 8th Impressionist exhibition.
Traveled to Panama and Martinique with Charles Laval.
Visited Arles in October, lived with Vincent van Gogh, and returned to Paris in late December.
Invited to the Les XX (The Twenty) exhibition.
Raised funds through the Hôtel Drouot auction in February, set sail for Tahiti on April 1, and married Teha'amana in November.
Displayed his Tahitian works at the Goupil gallery at the end of the year.
Left Tahiti on June 14, returned to France in August, and held a Durand-Ruel exhibition in November.
In early 1894, prepared woodcuts for his travelogue Noa Noa and separated from his wife Mette.
Attempted an Hôtel Drouot auction in February but failed; submitted the ceramic sculpture Oviri to the Salon in April, departed for Tahiti again on June 28, and arrived in September.
In April, heard the news of his daughter Aline's death and suffered from deteriorating health and financial difficulties.
Held an exhibition at the Vollard gallery in November.
Signed a supply contract with art dealer Ambroise Vollard.
Became the editor of the local newspaper Les Guêpes in February.
Resigned as editor of Les Guêpes in September and moved to Atuona on the island of Hiva Oa in the Marquesas Islands.
Died in the Marquesas Islands on May 8.
Contribution to Post-Impressionist and Symbolist Movements
As a French painter, sculptor, printmaker, ceramist, and writer, he made significant contributions to the Post-Impressionist and Symbolist movements.
Practice of Wood Engraving and Woodcut
An influential practitioner of wood engraving and woodcuts as an art form.
Pioneering the Synthetist Style
Recognized posthumously for his experimental use of color and Synthetist style that were distinct from Impressionism.
Masterpiece of Cloisonnism: The Yellow Christ
Created The Yellow Christ, a quintessential work of Cloisonnism, simplified with bold black outlines and flat areas of pure color.
Pioneer of Primitivism
The first artist to systematically use and achieve popular success with Primitivism, characterized by exaggerated body proportions, animal totems, geometric designs, and stark contrasts.
Influence on Modern Art
His bold, colorful, and design-oriented paintings inspired many modern artists, including Vincent van Gogh, Henri Matisse, and Pablo Picasso.
Completion of the Masterpiece Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?
Completed the large-scale painting Where Do We Come From? What Are We? Where Are We Going?, considered his masterpiece and artistic testament.
What art movements is Paul Gauguin associated with?
He is primarily associated with the Post-Impressionist and Symbolist movements, and is considered a pioneer of the Primitivist style.
What is the representative style of Paul Gauguin's works?
His works are characterized by experimental colors and the Synthetist style; influenced by Cloisonnism, he used bold outlines and flat areas of color.
What was Paul Gauguin's relationship with Vincent van Gogh?
He painted with Vincent van Gogh in Arles for 9 weeks in 1888, but their relationship deteriorated and they separated after the incident where Vincent cut off his own ear.
Why did Paul Gauguin leave for Tahiti?
He left for Tahiti to escape European civilization and 'everything that is artificial and conventional' in search of new artistic inspiration.
Was Paul Gauguin a successful painter during his lifetime?
